pattern: Both operations double the string's length regardless of type, so after i operations it always has exactly 2^i characters -- walk the operations backward, and at each step decide whether the target index falls in the untouched first half or the newly-appended (possibly shifted) second half, accumulating a shift count instead of ever materializing the string.
hints: Whatever the operation type, the word's length always exactly doubles -- after processing the first i operations its length is 2^i, so you never need to track lengths separately from the operation count.

Work backward from the last operation: if the target index lies in the first half of the word's length at that point, it's untouched by this operation and you can just move to the previous state; if it lies in the second half, subtract the half-length and, only if this operation was a shift, remember that one more shift will apply.
After unwinding every operation this way the index converges to 0 (the original 'a'), and the answer is 'a' advanced by the total number of shifts collected, modulo 26.

edge_inputs.description: all duplications, no shifts, answer stays 'a'
mix of duplicate and shift operations
k = 1 always resolves to the original 'a' regardless of operations
k at the very last position of the final word
shift count exceeds 26 and must wrap using modulo
single operation, only two characters exist
single duplication operation, k picks the copy

stress.description: long operation sequence with k ranging across the astronomically large final length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    length = max(2, min(size, 60))
    operations = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(length)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 2 ** length)
    return [k, operations]
